perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall take all practical steps to keep the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Informa tion confidential and shall restrict access to the Confidential Infor mation to those agents and professional advisers to whom disclosure is necessary in pursuit of the Approved Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall endeavor to take steps to keep the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information confidential and may restrict access to the Confidential Information to those agents and professional advisers.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall take all practical steps' to 'shall endeavor to take steps' introduces ambiguity, weakening the obligation to protect confidential information. Removing 'to whom disclosure is necessary in pursuit of the Approved Purpose' also makes the access restriction less defined.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11 provides a cause of action for unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. By weakening the obligation to protect confidential information, the modified text creates an ambiguity that could lead to a violation of this law, if the disclosure is considered an unfair trade practice. The original text provides a strong legal basis for confidentiality, while the modified text introduces uncertainty that could be exploited, making it less enforceable and potentially an unfair act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially violating M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11, as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text creates a strong legal basis for confidentiality, while the modified text introduces uncertainty, making it less enforceable and potentially an unfair act. The 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation' perturbation definition states that a legal contradiction happens when an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2 both refer to M.G.L c. 93A, § 11 as relating to 'actions for unfair trade practices'. By weakening the obligation to protect confidential information, which could lead to a disclosure considered an unfair trade practice, the modified text creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall inform all such employee s, agents and professional advisers of the provisions of this Agr eement and shall be responsible for ensuring compli ance with the said provisions by all agents and professional advi sers.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party should inform employees, agents and professional advisers of the provisions of this Agreement and is encouraged to promote awareness with the said provisions.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall inform' with 'should inform' and 'shall be responsible for ensuring compliance' with 'is encouraged to promote awareness' weakens the requirement for informing and ensuring compliance among employees, agents, and advisors. The original language has a legal obligation, but the modified language recommends an action that is not legally enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Trade Secret Law, M.G.L. c. 93 § 42
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts Trade Secret Law requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. By changing 'shall' to 'should' and 'shall be responsible for ensuring compliance' to 'is encouraged to promote awareness,' the modified text weakens the measures taken to protect trade secrets, potentially violating M.G.L. c. 93 § 42 if those measures are not deemed 'reasonable' by a court. The original text is stronger in the case of a trade secret being released.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha

As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets, potentially contradicting Massachusetts Trade Secret Law. The original text uses 'shall inform' and 'shall be responsible for ensuring compliance,' creating a mandatory obligation. The changed text uses 'should inform' and 'is encouraged to promote awareness,' which introduces ambiguity and reduces the legal enforceability of the requirement to protect trade secrets. The scraped law snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' which includes 'an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent'. By weakening the obligation to ensure employees, agents, and advisors are informed and compliant, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation, potentially violating the law. The changed text introduces ambiguity, making it more difficult to prove a breach of contract if a trade secret is disclosed. This falls under the 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction' definition, as the weakened language makes the legal obligation difficult to enforce, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that due to the sensitive nature of the Confidential Informati on, any breach of this Agreement would cause irreparable ha rm to the Disclosing Party and that the Disclosing Party shall therefore be entitled to equitable relief in additi on to all other remedies available at law.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that a breach of this Agreement may cause harm to the Disclosing Party and the Disclosing Party might seek remedies available at law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'would cause irreparable harm' to 'may cause harm' and 'shall therefore be entitled to equitable relief' to 'might seek remedies available at law' introduces significant ambiguity regarding the potential harm and the Disclosing Party's right to equitable relief. By changing shall to might, equitable relief is not guaranteed, making the agreement less effective.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act, M.G.L. c. 93 § 42
perturbation.law_explanation: The Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets, or actual or threatened misappropriation of trade secrets. By stating that harm 'may' occur and remedies 'might' be sought, the modified text weakens the certainty of harm and the Disclosing Party's ability to obtain equitable relief like an injunction, making it more difficult to pursue action under M.G.L. c. 93 § 42.

As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation of the Receiving Party regarding potential harm and the Disclosing Party's entitlement to remedies. The original text states a breach 'would cause irreparable harm' and the Disclosing Party 'shall therefore be entitled to equitable relief,' which creates a definitive obligation. The changed text uses 'may cause harm' and 'might seek remedies,' introducing ambiguity. The Law Explanation mentions that the Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act allows for injunctive relief. By changing 'shall' to 'might,' the certainty of obtaining equitable relief is undermined, creating a legal ambiguity that weakens the enforceability of the agreement under M.G.L. c. 93 § 42, as it relates to misappropriation. This falls under Ambiguity Legal Contradiction: introducing an obligation ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state law.

perturbation.original_text: All negotiating, drafting and presentations of offe rs shall be handled through ROI Corporation, as AGE NT FOR THE VENDOR, on ROI Corporation’s standard offer doc umentation and that I will seek independent legal a dvice for review prior to issuing my signature.
perturbation.changed_text: All negotiating, drafting and presentations of offers should be handled through ROI Corporation, and it is suggested that I seek independent legal advice for review prior to issuing my signature.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'should' and 'I will seek' to 'it is suggested that I seek' makes the process of negotiation and seeking legal advice non-mandatory. This weakens the Receiving Party's duty to use ROI Corporation and get legal advice, potentially making the agreement open to dispute. By changing mandatory to suggested language, it means one is now only recommended to do something.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Consumer Protection Act, M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the mandatory nature of handling negotiations through ROI Corporation and seeking independent legal advice, the modified text potentially creates an unfair or deceptive act or practice if ROI Corporation does not act in the best interest of both parties. The original text ensures more protection for the Receiving Party by requiring the use of a neutral intermediary (ROI Corporation) and independent legal review. Without these safeguards, the Receiving Party may be more vulnerable to unfair practices, which violates the spirit of M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2. This is because if ROI is not acting in the interest of both parties, then this is a deceptive practice that would create an illegal action.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall' to 'should' and from 'I will seek' to 'it is suggested that I seek' weakens the mandatory nature of the actions. The original text mandates using ROI Corporation and seeking legal advice, while the changed text makes these actions optional. The Law Explanation states this change 'potentially creates an unfair or deceptive act or practice if ROI Corporation does not act in the best interest of both parties' and suggests that without the original safeguards, the Receiving Party may be more vulnerable to unfair practices, which violates the spirit of M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2 both state that 'Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful.' The altered text increases the risk of unfair practices, thus contradicting the law.
